author: @MichaelSkinner-e9j
text:
That disk is essentially about as reflective as the little flake I used to clean the scanners off, Except it was almost like a silvery gray.

It was pretty fragile, but some bits were pretty hard, like I tried to use a paper clip or my nail to break some pieces. Or try to manipulate as much as I could because I didn’t have any way to measure any resistance.

Out of a fingernail, you might get a few points on it that are strong. Like I would say less than 10% of a fingernail,

replies.author: @Firstname137
replies.text:
EatOrLumby you can own a tank and drive it, you just have to make it road legal and have enough money. 
There was a local in my hometown who owns/owned allot of old military vehicles and hardware, he had a tank he would sometimes drive around.
You can also purchase a fighter jet if you'd like, but the cost of owning one and the maintenance required is incredibly high.
You could also purchase an old WW2 plane.
 I had the honor of meeting an incredibly amazing man who flew during the war, he used to escort bombers. 
He would fly a WW2 plane that was the same make and model as the one he had during the war, but I can't remember if it was personally his or a club he said he was a member of. 
I was in my early teens when I met him so my memory is a little vague on some details. 

So if you got the money you can buy pretty much anything.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
With overlapping plates and a air filled gel could absorb a lot of energy because the low mass would allow it to accelerate relative to the force applied to it, which would redistribute the energy into 'empty air' in essence, at least up until it either compressed the air or struck some kind of backing.

I was thinking of doing that with a titanium alloy with hexagonal interlocking plates sealed in a durable elastomer, but this graphene is much lighter and clearly less expensive, so it would most likely perform much better and be more affordable to produce.

 graphene /graphite is carbon, plastic sounds confusing

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
good points - the problem with plastic is it has both a general accepted definition and a narrower definition. 

From wikipedia

The word plastic is derived from the Greek πλαστικός (plastikos) meaning "capable of being shaped or molded", from πλαστός (plastos) meaning "molded". It refers to their malleability, or plasticity during manufacture, that allows them to be cast, pressed, or extruded into a variety of shapes—such as films, fibers, plates, tubes, bottles, boxes, and much more.
The common word plastic should not be confused with the technical adjective plastic, which is applied to any material which undergoes a permanent change of shape (plastic deformation) when strained beyond a certain point. Aluminum
 which is stamped or forged, for instance, exhibits plasticity in this 
sense, but is not plastic in the common sense; in contrast, in their 
finished forms, some plastics will break before deforming and therefore 
are not plastic in the technical sense.

author: @michaelsaumure7697
text: Well, a 231gr .45 ACP moving at 830 Ft/s has an equivalent Foot Pounds of force of about 850 I think, and the plastic thing breaks at 992 pounds, so it could stop that.  But  NATO standard 9mm has a much higher velocity on a lower mass, so I think that it would require more Graphene, or a thicker medium.

author: @Chris.Christophoros
text:
Awesome! I am truly amazed. I am goin to look now for some source of cheap gpahene powder, in Poland we are starting to make quite an industry in making graphene. I think that this reinforced plastic could be used for much more than bulletproof vests. Layered up to 5-10cm in thicknees (or more), it could be used as an additional armor for a armored personel carrier, a tank, helicopter or an anti-IED and landmine armor for patrol cars and trucks.

In addition, it would be great if manufacturing techniques could be open-source, so that small and medium companies all over the world could start producing press-molded parts, sheets, angle bars, pipes etc. from this composite. I fear that big corporations will soon grab this method and keep it for themselves.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Chris 3000 you are absolutley right mate it could be used for so much - good luck with your graphene venture

author: @rich1051414
text:
Great video. I was also confused by peoples ideas for future applications of graphene, which seem to get the micro and macro scale confused. Most of graphenes easy applications will be in electronics and possibly mechanics, but it is not a viable material to scale up to large scale applications to take advantage of its molecular arrangements. Unless of course someone figures out a way to produce continuous sheets of graphene...
That will be a whole new ballgame.

replies.author: @dedlunch
replies.text: it is a synergistic effect when putting it inside materials, as opposed to coating them:)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +dedlunch cool

author: @markanthonystringfellow3923

replies.author: @markanthonystringfellow3923
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Thanks, another idea I was thinking could be interesting would be make a 12"by11" rectangle or there-abouts 6mm thick walls with a cavity in the centre that can be filled with silicon nitride SI3N4 hexoids or standard spheres/balls, and somehow mix your graphene matrix in between. Ive found si3n4 much tougher than ruby, zirconium, silicon carbide etc, and still comparible to tungsten carbide in shock resistance, just a random thought lol!

author: @gary.richardson
text:
One of my favorite YouTube channels I subscribe to, Demolition Ranch, does test firings with many different fire arms and has a big following from his show. And, just recently he did a test firing on a new bullet proof vest sent to him by manufacturer with great results. If you have the means to make breastplate size thin sheets of your reinforced plastic and include about 20 of them then he may be able to do multiple firings to see how many sheets he can penetrate with different firearms.

https://www.youtube.com/user/DemolitionRanch

replies.text: I'm not sure what to say mate - if i state the problem you will think I am being obtuse but I don't mean to be - all you have to do is get some plastic, get some graphene and add the two - the challenge isn't on where to start but in the methodology of how to disperse the graphene - which is much more of a challenge than you would think - there are papers out there - so google scholar will serve you well - beyond that there is not a lot else I can say

author: @speedthought
text: Dear Robert, that you for you videos on Graphene, incredible and informative. For all us tinkers, home scientist, Graphene is by all accounts an incredible material that will change the world has we know it. Radical changes in building material, transportation, spaceships, armor, the lists are endless. Graphene is a wonder material that will allow us to drink the ocean doing away with the problem of 1% fresh water.  I enjoy your passion in the wonderful world of Graphene, that has been there for millions of years under our pencil tip and we thought nothing about it for just a few years ago.

author: @actudoran
text:
Top stuff Rob !  Entertaining as hell :) got my wheels and cogs turning like powered by rabid squirrels :)

about 15 years ago I was studying aero engineering and came up with a lifting body design with electrical field morphing wings ... that thermoplastic graphene combo could be a good material for structural parts as well as shell since cured by UV gets even tougher :) ... ah well, that was when I was pissed off by wing profile limitations for Ultralights ... something that stalls at 35 Knots won't ever be fast unless it morphs its profile ... tried the close proximity aerofoils effect for landing/take off config but still ... I thought it would get a tricky point in handling when in between configs ... loads of dirty air

Ah well ... back to work ... need to finish a forecast by the morning for our grand investors ... 

any ideea of melting point on that material ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: about 200 centigrade mate

Many big profs talk about such things but you actually have something in hand.. test it for corrosion and stability to environment, temp especially.. I'm sure people following you on youtube can help you in any way you want. I'm doing my phd and would love if I could be of help to you...
Congrats for this achievement.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

vijay deep bhatt
